document_index.title: Lab safety
document_index.excerpt: What to do if... There’s a chemical smell? Open the window straight away! Inform someone, either Minna or a senior lab member If you have a time sensitive experiment and cannot leave the lab, put on a gas mask and finish your work (found in drawers by the oven) If you know the source of the smell (i.e. It was your own xylene stained gloves) put the source in the fume hood and then open the window Examples of smelly chemicals: xylene is sickly sweet, strong acids are have a sharp sour stench, mercaptoethanol is bitter What to do if... You need to handle chemicals? Strong acids include sulphuric acid, hydrochloric acid and acetic acid. Only ever open the stock bottle in the fume hood, make sure the ventilation boost is on! Sick days, holidays (SoleTM, SAP HR) 
(In addition to notifying your PI about sick days and holidays): 
Each university employee with salary: Add your holidays and sick days to Sole TM at the latest on the first 
day of next month. For example, you were on holiday 3.-7.2. and sick leave 15.-16.2. You should also 
remember to confirm your working hours in SoleTM. You can do that by logging in 
(http://soletm.it.helsinki.fi) → Choose “Monthly sums and confirmation” → Click on the magnifying glass of

on holiday 3.-7.2. and sick leave 15.-16.2. You should also 
remember to confirm your working hours in SoleTM. You can do that by logging in 
(http://soletm.it.helsinki.fi) → Choose “Monthly sums and confirmation” → Click on the magnifying glass of 
the month → Check that your holidays and sick days are marked → Click “Return” → Click “Confirm” and 
then you can log out. 
If you do not add your holidays or sick days to SoleTM, your group will lose money, since the holidays and 
sick days are paid from another source. 
In addition, everyone with salary has to fill in the sick days to SAP HR https://saphr.it.helsinki.fi/irj/portal. 
If you are not a researcher, but support staff, you will also need to apply your holidays to SAP HR.

your lab manager/technician. The holiday period for research personnel is from 1.8. to 31.7. The number 
days off equivalent to holidays in that period is approximately 30 days (research personnel follows the 1600 
working hours per year schedule).

reports require a copy of any conference programs that you attend. Also, remember to ask for a travel 
insurance card before your trip from the coordinator. Save all the original receipts, boarding passes and 
official programs from any events that you attend during your trip. A good idea is to take photos of the 
receipts in case you lose the originals.

Rooms available for a quiet surrounding 
If you need a quiet working space, you can use the shared rooms A203a+b and A204b (2nd floor A-wing). In 
general, the tables cannot be booked and you need to empty them for the night. When you go for a break, 
you can lock your things in the lockers that are found in the rooms. There are a few tables that have been 
booked with special permission, the rest are free for everyone to use. The door to the A-wing spaces is 
normally open between 8-16.00. If you need to access the facilities later, you need to have RPU access rights 
(Biomedicum ID-card or a flexim-key). Currently due to COVID restrictions, the doors are kept locked. 
Rooms available for a meeting (booking is free of charge) 
Biomedicum meeting rooms available: Meeting room Kuutti (B328a1) can be booked with the Outlook 
calendar. Instructions can be found in Helpdesk webpages or by contacting the coordinator. In addition 
Pharmacology (B201a) and Anatomy (B101) meeting rooms can be booked by Anne Simola 
(anne.simola@helsinki.fi). 
The Medical campus library Terkko has quiet space to work in available for anyone to use (not open for the 
summer). It’s also possible to book rooms from Terkko: https://flamma.helsinki.fi/en/group/tilat/opetus-ja-
kokoustilojen-varaaminen

Lab meetings (Research coordinator)
Lab meeting is once a week for 1.5hours. Schedule can be found here: Lab Meetings 2026 It is recommended to be on site unless you have a pressing need to be elsewhere. The meeting timeslot and location varies, details are found in the lab google calendar and the google sheet linked above. If you attend the lab meeting remote, also mark your remote status to Lab Meetings 2026, under the column “Remote” so the group knows of the need to setup the remote connections. The Zoom link for the lab meetings can be found in the Google Calendar, lab meeting agenda, and the schedule.
In the beginning of each Lab meeting we will have a 30 minutes General lab meeting with a preplanned Agenda, which can be found here: Farkkilab meeting general agendas and notes. If you have suggestions that you would like to discuss, ask Joonas to add it to the agenda. 
The Lab meeting program is coordinated by Research Coordinator (Joonas). It consists of one or two 30 minutes slots including the presentation and discussion. The schedule for the presentations can be found at Lab Meetings 2026. Lab meeting presentations are meant to be discussive, and allow for getting insights from the lab members to your project. It is especially encouraged to present project plans, concepts, technologies, ideas, challenges, preliminary data etc. which can benefit from feedback and not just fully ready results or projects.
The lab meeting slots are allocated during the end of the previous season, and loosely follow the presentation order of the previous season. The aim is for every member to present at least once per season. If you need to change your slot, contact Joonas - always as soon as possible.

Hosting Journal clubs Reviewer Club (TRC)
We use every second lab meeting to review relevant articles. There is always a host appointed who selects an article and leads the discussion during this meeting. For further information on how to host see the following document: Guidelines how to host a Third Reviewer Club or TRC
Infrastructure (IT) meetings (IT specialist)
The IT meeting, coordinated by Deb, is held on an as-needed basis every 1-2 months. The meeting focuses on data and project organisation and management. 
Meeting is held as a working lunch serving pizza at 12-13, and EVERYONE should attend.
The meeting also instructs how to use different resources including CSC services for our data analysis workflows and identifying opportunities to further improve our research pipelines. All computational updates, issues, and proposed enhancements are shared and discussed to support continuous development of our computational processes.
A dedicated Slack channel #IT-meeting is available for ongoing discussions, meeting announcements, and follow-up communication related to IT and computational topics.
Cyclops meetings (spatial biology manager)
CyclOps (Cyclic operatives) meeting is hosted by Saun. It is a once a month meeting that includes discussion and suggestions to improve our tCycIF, GeoMX and Xenium operations. It is recommended for all the lab members who have planned or are planning to do tCycIF, GeoMX,Xenium experiments in the future. We also give updates on new methods, new validated antibodies, new instrument options available for these experiments.

We also give updates on new methods, new validated antibodies, new instrument options available for these experiments. We have a dedicated #cyclops-meeting channel in slack for discussions and announcements about the meeting.
Computational troubleshooting (CT) meetings (Sara Palomino)
The CT meeting is hosted by Sara. It is a weekly, on-demand meeting aimed at discussing everyday computational challenges we encounter in our projects — for example, a script with persistent errors, a plot we are not satisfied with, an incompatible library, or any other issue we have been struggling to solve.
The meeting is also a space to share and discuss new computational tools, packages, or workflows that could benefit our ongoing analyses. Although the CT meeting is mainly recommended for computationally oriented members, all lab members are welcome to join and contribute.
Each week, we ask if anyone would like to present or bring a topic for discussion, and the meeting is scheduled accordingly. We also have a dedicated Slack channel, #computational_troubleshooting, for sharing announcements, materials, and follow-up discussions related to the meeting.

WET LAB
To every new wet lab member: Onboarding info & tour around the facilities with the lab coordinator. 
General wet lab practices 
Safety and protective equipment
Safety comes first. Protective equipment is available and must be used when handling hazardous chemicals or biological materials. This includes gloves, lab coat, eye protection, goggles, an apron, cryoprotective gloves, and face mask when needed.
You need to know when to use gloves, a lab coat, and other protective gear, when to work in the fume hood, and when sterile work in the laminar hood is required. Remember to switch the fume hood on before use.
If you do not know where to find safety items, ask people in the lab. More information about safety equipment can be found in the ONCOSYS orientation document: 2021_ONCOSYS_Orientation_by_Karen_Ahlnas.pdf
Always ask when in doubt. There should be no threshold for asking. 
Labeling reagents, solutions, and samples
No chemicals, powders, solutions, reagents, or samples should be left unmarked.
Always label the following information:
name of the substance, solution, reagent, or sample
molarity or percentage
solvent, when applicable
date prepared
your name or initials
Do not write directly on glassware. Use paper tape for writing.
Even Eppendorf tubes or Falcon tubes containing water for balancing the centrifuge must be labeled directly on the tube.
Shared items and equipments
Keep in mind that multiple people use the same materials, resources, and equipment. 
Always return items to their correct places after use to ensure also others can find the items. 
Empty items from the cell culture room should be returned to the wet lab room, to the coordinator's bench.

Working with Human-Derived Material
When working with human-derived material, such as patient samples, the Oncosys-OVA confidentiality agreement must be signed before handling any samples. Ask the Lab coordinator or Clinical team coordinator for help with this.
Remember that human-derived material always carries a potential risk of infection. Wear a lab coat and gloves, and disinfect surfaces and labware after finishing your work using Virkon.
Be extra careful when using scalpels, as they are very sharp. Discard scalpels and syringe needles in the yellow sharp waste containers.
Hepatitis B vaccination is recommended when working with fresh patient samples. It can be obtained through occupational healthcare at Mehiläinen and should be compensated by your employer. People working on grants should discuss this with their supervisor.
Cell Culture Rules
Biological waste disposal
Biological waste must be placed in the designated biological waste containers. Biological waste includes all materials that have been in contact with cells, cell culture reagents, chemicals, and disposable plasticware that has been in direct contact with cells or reagents.
For example, empty tip boxes are not considered biological waste unless they are contaminated, such as after a spill. Used pipette tips are always considered biological waste.

All non-biological waste in the cell culture room can be placed in the regular waste bin under the sink.
Laminar hoods
Book the hood using the calendar on each laminar hood.
In general, try to use our own laminar hood. If needed, you can book other hoods.
When using another lab’s laminar hood, take our lab’s disposables with you, such as tips and tubes. After finishing your work, take all our lab materials out of the hood. Always leave the hood in the same or better condition than when you started.
When you finish working in the hood: 
Clean the hood after use.
Refill the disposables if something has run out. It is good practise to turn off the laminar hood light when not in use. 
Rinse the aspirator tube with EtOH, and close the pump.
Put the pipette boy for charging within the hood. Turn on the electricity to the socket in the laminar hood screen if it is off.
Turn off the laminar hood light. 
If you are the last user of the day in the laminar hood:
Close the hood. 
If you are the last person working in the cell culture room:
Turn off the water bath.
Turn off the big centrifuge.
Turn off the lights.

OUTBOARDING
Go through all the materials and files you generated: wet lab -plates, test tubes, slides, etc.; files.
Trash what you think will not be needed/cannot be used later. 
Delete unnecessary files. Important files - have them saved in the correct folder, with clear naming. Ask your supervisor for instructions where to store your files. See instructions on IT outboarding.
Return lab coats to Saun/Anastasia.
Return the keys to BMH Info receptionists– to the key responsible person.
Return your computer and IT equipment.
Lab manager will remove you from Slack, mailing list, Benchling, Wiki, Google Drive, Calendars, other clouds, and Drives. If you still need access to some of these files, please let us know, otherwise your access will be removed by default. Ask Joonas for the extended access. 
IT outboarding 
Be mindful of your timeline and make sure that you allocate time to clean and organise your data. 
Before you return your computer, make sure it’s cleaned and your important files are backed up - if you are unsure what to keep, ask for help.
Backups should not exist only on physical hard drives, but also in the cloud - csc-allas, datacloud, etc.
Ensure your project log file is up to date, and legible for everyone else too
Save the files which are important - and add in links between project log and the locations of important files:
Final results 
Publication material 
Files which are for future use for someone in the lab 
Organise your files:
Store them in the correct storage place, in the right folders (usually organised by projects). For guidelines see section Resources - Storage. 
Store your large files which are unlikely to be used actively in the future on the cold storage spaces.

Change in the legal review of MTA contract documents concerning the research activities of the Faculty of Medicine
The Faculty of Medicine has launched an operating model to make communication between faculty researchers and research administration lawyers more flexible regarding the legal review of MTA research contract documents. The process applies to new MTA contracts that require legal review.
 Established and already approved MTA contract templates (e.g. Addgene, ATCC) can be delivered directly to admin-meilahti@helsinki.fi, who will take care of signatures by the head of the unit and the signed contract will be delivered to the researcher.
For MTA contracts that require legal review, proceed as follows:
Researchers submit documents requiring legal review directly to the service address of research lawyers (tutkimuksenjuristit@helsinki.fi).
Once the contract has passed the legal examination, the lawyers send the document to the researcher.
The researcher sends the document to be signed to the faculty's service address admin-meilahti@helsinki.fi and also to the lawyer responsible for the legal audit (firstname.lastname@helsinki.fi). The lawyer responsible for the review acknowledges that the document can proceed to signatures.
The general administration takes care of the contract signed by the head of the unit for the researcher.

## Slide 1
Lab safety

## Slide 2
What to do if... There’s a chemical smell?
Open the window straight away!
Inform someone, either Minna or a senior lab member
If you have a time sensitive experiment and cannot leave the lab, put on a gas mask and finish your work (found in drawers by the oven)
If you know the source of the smell (i.e. It was your own xylene stained gloves) put the source in the fume hood and then open the window
Examples of smelly chemicals: xylene is sickly sweet, strong acids are have a sharp sour stench, mercaptoethanol is bitter

## Slide 3
What to do if... You need to handle chemicals?
Acids/alkali
Strong acids include sulphuric acid, hydrochloric acid and acetic acid. Only ever open the stock bottle in the fume hood, make sure the ventilation boost is on! Use double gloves or chemical safety gloves to handle them. Use the plastic 5ml pipettes to transfer the needed amount to your container. Remember! Never add water to acid! The water/diluent should go first, then add the acid on top of it. 
Strong bases include potassium hydroxide and sodium hydroxide. They usually come in powdered form. Handle them in the fume hood with double gloves. Do not use metal spoons to measure out the powder, they will corrode. Use plastic instead. 
Strong acids and bases are hazardous chemical waste, if you want to dispose of large amounts of it, give it to Minna. If you have mixed it in with other compounds and the amounts are very small (i.e. A test tube where an acid was just one component) then it goes in the general waste bin. 
Check the waste management guide on the freezer door if you are unsure what to do

## Slide 4
What to do if... You need to handle chemicals?

## Slide 4
What to do if... You need to handle chemicals?
Organic solvents
Xylene is long-chained hydrocarbon that we use for paraffin removal to stain tissue sections. It is colorless but with a strong sweet odour, it is a carcinogen both through inhalation and absorption through the skin. Only ever open the bottles in the fume hood. After you’ve closed the bottle, leave it in the fume hood so any traces around the cap have evaporated. Change gloves frequently when you work with xylene BUT leave the used gloves in a corner in the fume hood, otherwise the trace amounts of xylene on the gloves will stink up the lab.
Xylene is hazardous chemical waste, for deparaffinization we reuse the chemical, so pour it back into the stock bottle. If you want to replace the chemical with fresh xylene then pour the old solvents into the dark xylene waste bottles under the fume hood. 
Chloroform, phenol and mercaptoethanol should be handled the same as xylene but milder alcohols like isopropanol and ethanol can be handled on the bench. Note that they are still hazardous chemical waste due to how flammable they are.

## Slide 5
What to do if... There’s a spill?
If you know what the chemical is, you can clean it up yourself, if you don’t know then ask Minna or a senior lab member for help
If the spill is a mild buffer or reagent from a kit then wipe it up with paper towels and clean the bench/floor with some ethanol spray
If you spilled a strong acid/base or organic solvent in the fume hood then absorb the spill with paper towels and place the used towels in the fume hood to dry

If you spilled a strong acid/base or organic solvent in the fume hood then absorb the spill with paper towels and place the used towels in the fume hood to dry
If you spilled a strong acid/base or organic solvent on the bench or floor then open the window first, if the spill is very large then put on a gas mask, then wipe up the spill with paper towels and place them in the fume hood. Clean the bench/floor with ethanol and wipe again

## Slide 6
What to do if... You need to handle chemicals?
Toxins
If you are handling a poisonous substance such a cisplatin or cholera toxin you should only ever open the container in a laminar or fume hood. Change gloves immediately after you’ve used it.
If you are using this poison in cell culture media collect the toxic media into a container, do not collect it in the suction bottle.
Do not use your phone, check your watch or touch your face or hair when you are handling poisons in the cell culture room. If a phone is needed to take photos then place your phone in a ziplock bag and use it in the hood, then dispose of the ziplock back when you are done.

## Slide 7
Other symbols
Compressed gas: do not attempt to exchange a CO2 bottle by yourself, send a request in Granlund (from flamma) to have a professional handle it
Oxidizing or explosive compounds are extremely dangerous and should not be handled in our lab, hopefully you’ll never see these two symbols

## Slide 8
What to do if... There’s a fire?
If you caused the fire with your own bunsen burner and it’s just your own notebook burning on the bench then you can extinguish it yourself by dousing it with water.

## Slide 8
What to do if... There’s a fire?
If you caused the fire with your own bunsen burner and it’s just your own notebook burning on the bench then you can extinguish it yourself by dousing it with water. Turn off the gas for the bunsen and always keep a 1L bottle of water on your bench when operating it.
In all other cases evacuate immediately.

## Slide 9
Occupational safety
As undergraduate students you are insured by the University when doing studies, training or internships at labs. Insurance provider Pohjola Vakuutus
PhD students, post-docs and technicians with an employment relationship with the university are covered by occupational healthcare provided by Mehiläinen
If you have had a workplace accident you can visit any healthcare provider and have the cost of care covered by the university using our insurance number 78-20000-06870-8 and our y-tunnus 0313471-7, remember to say that it is a workplace accident
https://flamma.helsinki.fi/en/group/henkilostoasiat/occupational-safety

## Slide 11
What to do if... You’ve got something in your eye?
Go to the emergency shower near the break area or use the eyewash bottles in room B314b
Then contact the occupational healthcare 010 414 0666 or go to the emergency services next door
If it was a chemical spill, tell the healthcare providers what chemical it was
If it was biological material, say that you may have been exposed to blood-borne contaminants
These same instructions apply if you got something in your mouth
If you had a spill on your skin, such as hands, then you should rinse the area in a sink, or if the spill is extensive enough, in the emergency shower.

Takehome message
Always check the chemicals you will work with and make sure you understand the risks associated with them and how to handle spills and waste disposal for that particular chemical
You can check the safety data sheet in the network drive P:\h304\kauppi_shared\Minna_kp\inventory\MSDS
